segments.text: Genetic Manipulation of the Mouse

Mouse Genetics
Mutations can be created by standard mutagenesis, then selection
Genes can be introduced into embryos or embryonic stem cells generating:
1)  Dominant expression-all cells
2)  Knockouts-recessive, must be 			     	homozygous
3)  Tissue-specific knockouts or 	  	 		activations

Nobel 2007 The Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine 2007
"for their discoveries of principles for introducing specific gene modifications in mice by the use of embryonic stem cells".

Mouse Genetic Engineering
Dominant expression
2)  Knockouts-recessive, must be homozygous
3)  Tissue-specific knockouts or activations
Genes can be introduced into embryos or eggs
Mouse transformation

Transgenic
Any individual carrying a foreign DNA that researchers have inserted into the genome of an organism

Metallothionein promoter
Transgenic mice were fed a diet supplemented with zinc which induces metallothionein promoter in the liver instead of pituitary gland where growth hormone is normally made. The amount of growth hormone secreted correlated with the size of these mice. The transgenic mice became enormous, up to 80 percent larger than their normal littermates.

Mouse with rat growth hormone
+ rgh            wild-type sibling

Mouse Genetic Engineering
1)  Dominant expression
2)  Knockouts-recessive, must be homozygous. (Requires being able to select for specific insertions)
3)  Tissue-specific knockouts or activations
Genes can be introduced into embryonic stem cells

Embryonic Stem Cells
Cells derived from the inner cell mass of the early mammalian embryo that can give rise to all the cells in the body.  It can be grown in culture, genetically modified and inserted into a blastocyst to develop a transgenic animal

Embryonic Stem Cells
Embryonic stem cells are totipotent

Totipotent
Description of a cell state during early embryonic development in which the cells have not yet differentiated and retain the ability to produce every type of cell found in the developing embryo and adult animal

Mouse Genetic Engineering
1)  Dominant expression
2)  Knockouts-recessive, must be homozygous
3)  Tissue-specific knockouts or activations

Knockouts
What are they?
Transgenics with specific gene removed
How are they made?
Mutant is constructed in vitro
Embryonic cells not taking up mutant construct 	are killed
Embryonic cells not taking up mutant by 	recombining into target (the construct has 	integrated somewhere else) are killed.

Knockout  Vector  (part 1)
-Insert neomycinR gene into
the coding region of the gene that is getting “knocked out”
neoR gene interrupts normal coding sequence, thus knocking it out
-neoR cells will grow in G418, a neomycin analog

neoR
neoR
tk

Knockout  Vector (part 2)
-note the herpes tk gene on targeting vector
-Ganciclovir, (Gancyclovir) will kill cells carrying the tk gene

How Gancyclovir Kills:
Deoxyguanosine analog
Normal thymidine kinase in
mammalian cells will not
recognize GCV as a substrate
Herpes Symplex Virus thymidine kinase thinks it’s a substrate, so it converts it into a monophosphate.   The second phosphate is added and it causes DNA chain termination during DNA synthesis

Knockout:
Transform altered DNA into
Mouse embryonic stem cells
-note that the mouse is agouti

Vector Fates
(Part 1)
Cells are grown in G418 and Ganciclovir
Cells die because no neor gene is present
neor

Vector Fates
(Part 2)
Cells are grown in G418 and Ganciclovir
Cell die because tk gene is present
tk

Vector Fates
(Part 3)
Cells are grown in G418 and Ganciclovir
Cells live because neor gene is present
and because tk gene is absent
neoR
neoR
tk
Summary of knockout vector fates

Examples of Mouse Knockouts
Telomerase deficient mice
Myostatin deficient mice
trp2 deficient mice
